Were You Asked This?
Design a Server Health Monitoring System

Design a distributed health monitoring system that can track the status and performance metrics of millions of services, detecting potential issues before they cause failures and providing real-time alerts to operators.

Asked at:

Google

Amazon

A server health monitoring system is an observability platform that collects heartbeats, metrics, and logs from services; evaluates their health; and raises real-time alerts when something goes wrong. Think of products like Prometheus, Datadog, and Amazon CloudWatch—operators use them to see dashboards, investigate anomalies, and get paged before incidents escalate.

Interviewers ask this to test if you can design high-ingest, low-latency, fault-tolerant systems for millions of sources. They’re probing your ability to handle event streams at scale, control cardinality and cost, separate hot vs. cold paths, deliver timely and deduplicated alerts, and design for multi-tenancy and multi-region resilience. You should demonstrate clear trade-offs (push vs. pull, pre-aggregation vs. raw storage, at-least-once vs. exactly-once) and an end-to-end plan from agents to alert delivery.

Community Solutions

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to collect health signals (heartbeats, metrics) from millions of services and see current status in near real time.

Users should be able to define alerting rules and thresholds (including anomaly or SLO-based alerts) and receive timely, deduplicated notifications.

Users should be able to explore dashboards and run ad-hoc queries on recent and historical time-series data.

Users should be able to manage services, group them by teams/environments, and control alert routing, silence windows, and escalation policies.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How would you ingest and store telemetry from millions of services while controlling write throughput, label cardinality, and cost?

How can you deliver real-time alerts with low end-to-end latency (e.g., under 60 seconds) and high reliability during traffic spikes?

How would you support interactive dashboards and ad-hoc queries over billions of time-series points without timing out?

How do you ensure high availability and disaster recovery so alerts still fire during a regional outage?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Scaling Writes

The core challenge is ingesting high-volume metric samples and heartbeats from millions of services. You must design a durable, partitioned write path with backpressure, quotas, and cost controls, plus rollups to keep storage sustainable.

Read More
Real-time Updates

Operators need sub-minute alerts and live dashboards. A streaming evaluation pipeline, low-latency fanout to notification channels, and separating hot vs. cold paths are essential to provide timely updates during incidents.

Read More
Dealing with Contention

Hot partitions appear when many hosts report the same metric or when a few tenants generate extreme cardinality. You must spread load via sharding, enforce label limits, and avoid hotspots in both ingestion and query paths.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

Kafka provides a durable, horizontally scalable buffer between edge ingestion and downstream processing. It smooths bursts, enables backpressure, and lets you independently scale alert evaluators and time-series writers.

Read More
Flink

Flink excels at stateful stream processing with windowed aggregations and exactly-once/at-least-once semantics. It’s a natural fit for continuous rule evaluation, anomaly detection, and building derived metrics in real time.

Read More
Cassandra

Cassandra’s wide-row, time-series-friendly data model and tunable consistency suit high-write workloads with time-bucketed partitions and TTLs. It’s commonly used to store recent metrics with rollups and multi-DC replication.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Ad Click Aggregator

Both systems handle massive event streams, windowed aggregations, and near real-time insights. The ingestion pipeline, stream processing, and cost control patterns map closely to metrics collection and alert evaluation.

Read
Practice
Rate Limiter

Sliding windows, counters, and per-tenant isolation are central to both designs. Strategies to avoid hot keys, enforce quotas, and maintain low-latency state are directly applicable to metrics cardinality control and alert thresholds.

Read
Practice
FB Live Comments

Delivering low-latency updates to many subscribers mirrors sending alerts and updating dashboards in real time. Techniques for fanout, deduplication, and resilience under spikes carry over.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Ignoring metrics label/cardinality explosion

Unbounded labels (e.g., user_id or request_id) will blow up memory and storage, degrade query performance, and inflate costs. Interviewers expect explicit controls: limits, normalization, pre-aggregation, and quotas.

Coupling alerting to the storage write path

If alerts depend on successful writes or query reads, storage hiccups will delay pages exactly when you need them most. Decoupling via a streaming evaluation path ensures alerts fire even when storage is degraded.

Single-region or single-broker architecture

Centralized components create brittle SPOFs; a regional outage would blind your monitoring and suppress alerts. You should propose multi-region replication, stateless workers with checkpointing, and redundant notification channels.
